resources.string: What is a Stack?

📚 WHAT IS A STACK?

A Stack is a linear data structure that follows the principle:

👉 LIFO (Last In, First Out)
The last element inserted is the first one to be removed.

📌 Real-Life Examples:
• Stack of plates 🍽️ - You add/remove from the top
• Undo feature in apps - Last action is undone first
• Browser history - Back button shows last visited page
• Function calls - Recursion uses call stack

A Stack is a conceptual structure consisting of homogeneous elements based on the principle of last in first out (LIFO). It's a common data structure used in many areas of computer science.
Basic Operations

🔹 BASIC OPERATIONS IN STACK

1. PUSH - Insert an element into the stack
• Adds element to the TOP
• Time Complexity: O(1)

2. POP - Remove the top element
• Removes the most recently added element
• Time Complexity: O(1)

3. PEEK/TOP - View the top element
• Returns top element without removing it
• Time Complexity: O(1)

4. isEmpty - Check if stack is empty
• Returns true if no elements

5. isFull - Check if stack is full
• For array implementation only
• Returns true if capacity is reached
Stack Rules & Concepts

🔹 STACK RULES

1. Only one end (TOP) is used for both insertion and deletion
• You cannot add/remove from the middle or bottom

2. Push & Pop operations happen at TOP only
• All modifications are at the TOP

3. Cannot access middle elements directly
• Must pop elements from top to access others

4. Overflow
• Occurs when trying to push to a full stack
• Memory limit exceeded (array implementation)

5. Underflow
• Occurs when trying to pop from an empty stack
• No elements to remove

🔹 STACK REPRESENTATIONS:
✅ Using Array - Fixed size, faster access, may overflow
✅ Using Linked List - Dynamic size, no overflow until memory full
Time Complexity

⏱️ TIME COMPLEXITY ANALYSIS

Stack Operations Performance:

Operation | Time Complexity | Space
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Push | O(1) | O(n)
Pop | O(1) | O(n)
Peek/Top | O(1) | O(1)
Search | O(n) | -
Access | O(n) | -
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Why is Push O(1)?
→ Inserting at a fixed position (TOP)
→ No need to shift elements

Why is Pop O(1)?
→ Removing from a fixed position (TOP)
→ No reorganization needed

Why is Search O(n)?
→ Must pop all elements to find a value
→ Cannot access middle directly
Real-World Applications

💡 APPLICATIONS OF STACK

1. Expression Evaluation & Conversion
• Evaluating infix expressions
• Converting infix to postfix notation
• Calculating mathematical expressions

2. Parenthesis/Bracket Checking
• Validating balanced parentheses: ( { [ ] } )
• Compiler syntax checking

3. Function Calls (Call Stack)
• Managing function calls in recursion
• Call stack trace in debuggers
• Program execution flow

4. Undo/Redo Operations
• Undo in text editors (Word, Google Docs)
• Undo in design software (Photoshop, Figma)
• Browser back button

5. Backtracking Algorithms
• Maze solving (DFS)
• Puzzle solving
• Game AI decision making

6. Memory Management
• Memory allocation on stack vs heap
• Variable scope management

7. Browser History
• Storing visited pages
• Back navigation
Step-by-Step Examples

🔹 STEP-BY-STEP EXAMPLES

📌 PUSH Operation:
Initial: [10, 20, 30]
TOP = 2

After Push(40):
TOP → [40]
 [30]
 [20]
 [10]

📌 POP Operation:
Initial:
TOP → [40]
 [30]
 [20]
 [10]

After Pop():
Removed: 40
TOP → [30]
 [20]
 [10]

📌 PEEK Operation:
Stack: [10, 20, 30]
Peek() → Returns 30 (without removing)

💡 BONUS: Implementation Tips
→ Use dynamic arrays/lists for flexibility
→ Handle overflow/underflow gracefully
→ Add animations for better visualization
→ Create step-by-step simulation mode

🔹 PUSH OPERATION (Insertion)

Algorithm:
1. Check if stack is full
2. If full → Overflow error
3. Else → increment TOP pointer
4. Insert element at TOP position

Time Complexity: O(1)

Example:
Initial: [10, 20, 30], TOP = 2
Push(40):
 • Increment TOP to 3
 • Add 40 at position 3
Result: [10, 20, 30, 40], TOP = 3

Key Points:
• Always adds to the top
• No shifting of elements needed
• Constant time operation

🔹 POP OPERATION (Deletion)

Algorithm:
1. Check if stack is empty
2. If empty → Underflow error
3. Else → remove element from TOP
4. Decrement TOP pointer

Time Complexity: O(1)

Example:
Initial: [10, 20, 30, 40], TOP = 3
Pop():
 • Remove element at TOP (40)
 • Decrement TOP to 2
Result: [10, 20, 30], TOP = 2

Key Points:
• Always removes from the top
• Returns removed element
• No reorganization needed

🔹 PEEK OPERATION (Access)

Algorithm:
1. Check if stack is empty
2. If empty → return null/error
3. Else → return element at TOP
4. Do NOT decrement TOP

Time Complexity: O(1)

Example:
Stack: [10, 20, 30], TOP = 2
Peek(): Returns 30

Stack remains: [10, 20, 30]
TOP unchanged at 2

Key Points:
• Only views the element
• Does not modify the stack
• No elements removed
Stack Overflow! Cannot push - stack is full
Stack Underflow! Cannot pop - stack is empty
Stack is empty
Stack is full
LIFO: Last In, First Out - The last element added is the first to be removed
Overflow: Attempting to push an element to a full stack
Underflow: Attempting to pop an element from an empty stack
TOP: Pointer indicating the position of the most recently added element
